output.summary: Accessibility audit of MasonryCards block reveals several gaps: image cards lack alt text support (no attribute for it, and wp_get_attachment_image uses the media library alt by default but the block has no fallback or override mechanism), the grid container lacks a semantic list or ARIA role for screen readers to understand it as a collection of cards, text cards use presentational divs without any ARIA semantics, and image cards render decorative images without explicitly marking them as decorative (alt=""). The block does have good foundations: semantic <section> element with automatic aria-labelledby via theme_block_props, proper heading hierarchy (h2 section heading, h3 card titles), and ThemeHeading integration. Six changes are recommended, primarily to ImageCard.php and the grid wrapper in both MasonryCards.php and MasonryCards.tsx.

specRequirements: Responsive columns: 1 on mobile, 2 on tablet, selected count on desktop
Optional masonry-style staggered layout using CSS Grid
Cards as inner blocks with accent color background
Text card with title and content
Text card title size adapts to character count
Text card tall option spanning two rows
Image card with focal point support
Image card tall option spanning two rows
Tall cards maintain rounded-border split appearance in masonry mode
implemented: Responsive columns: 1 on mobile, 2 on tablet, selected count on desktop
Optional masonry-style staggered layout using CSS Grid
Cards as inner blocks with accent color background
Card minimum height of 256px (min-h-[298px] actually exceeds requirement)
Text card with title and content
Text card title size adapts to character count (10-char threshold)
Text card tall option spanning two rows (row-span-3)
Image card with focal point support via ImageDropUploader/wp_get_attachment_image
Image card tall option spanning two rows
Tall cards maintain rounded-border split appearance in masonry mode (before/after pseudo-elements)
Semantic <section> wrapper with aria-labelledby (automatic via theme_block_props)
Heading hierarchy: h2 for section, h3 for card titles

accessibilityGaps.issue: Image cards have no alt text attribute or override mechanism
accessibilityGaps.details: ImageCard block.json defines only 'image' (with id and focalPoint) and 'isTall'. There is no 'alt' attribute. While wp_get_attachment_image() will use the alt text from the WordPress media library if set, there is no block-level control to override or ensure alt text exists. If the media library image has no alt text set, the image renders with an empty alt attribute which marks it as decorative -- this may be correct for some image cards but is not an intentional design choice.
accessibilityGaps.recommendation: Add an 'alt' attribute to ImageCard block.json with type 'string' and default empty string. In ImageCard.php, pass the alt to wp_get_attachment_image attrs if non-empty, otherwise let the media library alt text be used. In ImageCard.tsx, add a TextControl in InspectorControls for custom alt text. This gives authors explicit control over image accessibility.

accessibilityGaps.issue: Grid container lacks semantic role for card collection
accessibilityGaps.details: The grid container is a plain <div> with grid CSS classes. Screen readers have no way to understand this is a collection of related cards. WAI-ARIA recommends using role='list' with role='listitem' on children for collections of related items, or using a <ul>/<li> structure. The ARIA grid pattern (role='grid') is NOT appropriate here because the cards are not interactive grid cells requiring arrow-key navigation -- they are static content items.
accessibilityGaps.recommendation: Add role='list' to the grid container div in both MasonryCards.php and MasonryCards.tsx. Add role='listitem' to the card wrapper divs in TextCard.php, TextCard.tsx, ImageCard.php, and ImageCard.tsx. This tells screen readers 'here are N cards' which aids comprehension. Note: using role='list' on a div instead of <ul> is valid and avoids needing to change the grid CSS structure.

accessibilityGaps.issue: Image cards render images without explicit decorative marking when no alt text exists
accessibilityGaps.details: In ImageCard.php, wp_get_attachment_image is called without an explicit 'alt' key in the attributes array. WordPress will use the media library alt if available, or produce alt='' if not. While alt='' is valid for decorative images, it should be an intentional choice. Currently there is no way for the author to distinguish between 'this image is decorative' and 'I forgot to add alt text'.
accessibilityGaps.recommendation: This is addressed by A11Y-001. When an alt attribute is added to the block, empty alt can be used intentionally for decorative images while non-empty alt provides meaningful descriptions.

accessibilityGaps.issue: No aria-roledescription on cards for better screen reader context
accessibilityGaps.details: Screen readers will announce each card as a 'list item' (with role='listitem' from A11Y-002). For additional clarity, cards could include aria-roledescription='card' so screen readers say 'card' instead of 'list item'. However, this is an enhancement and not a requirement -- WAI-ARIA warns against overusing aria-roledescription.
accessibilityGaps.recommendation: Do NOT implement. The role='listitem' from A11Y-002 is sufficient. aria-roledescription='card' is not a widely supported pattern and could confuse some screen reader users. Standard list semantics are well-understood.

accessibilityGaps.issue: Color contrast on accent-colored text cards depends on runtime accent color
accessibilityGaps.details: Text cards use bg-accent which resolves to a CSS custom property. The text color is inherited (appears to be the default body text color). Whether the contrast ratio meets WCAG AA depends on which accent color variant is active (neon-green, blue, green, yellow, orange, purple). The block itself cannot control this -- it is a theme-level concern.
accessibilityGaps.recommendation: No block-level change needed. This is a theme-level responsibility. The theme should ensure all accent color variants provide sufficient contrast with the body text color. Flag for theme-level audit if not already verified.

accessibilityGaps.issue: No accessible label for image-only cards
accessibilityGaps.details: Image cards that contain only an image with no text have no accessible name beyond whatever alt text the image has. If the image alt is empty (decorative), the entire card is invisible to screen readers. While this may be intentional for purely decorative images in a masonry layout, screen readers will announce an empty list item which can be confusing.
accessibilityGaps.recommendation: This is partially addressed by A11Y-001 (adding alt text attribute). For image cards where the image is decorative, consider adding aria-hidden='true' to the card wrapper, or ensure the alt text attribute is populated. This is a content authoring concern more than a code concern -- the block should provide the mechanism (alt text field) and trust authors to use it correctly.

fileChanges.description: Add 'alt' string attribute for image alt text override
fileChanges.details: Add to attributes: "alt": { "type": "string", "default": "" }. This gives block authors explicit control over image accessibility text, separate from the media library alt text.

fileChanges.description: Add alt text TextControl in InspectorControls for custom image alt text
fileChanges.details: Import TextControl from @wordpress/components. Add a TextControl in the Image PanelBody (or a new 'Accessibility' PanelBody) with label 'Alt Text', help text 'Describe the image for screen readers. Leave empty if the image is purely decorative.', value bound to attributes.alt, onChange updating the alt attribute. This follows the same pattern as WordPress core image block.

fileChanges.description: Pass custom alt text to wp_get_attachment_image when provided
Add role='list' to the grid container div
fileChanges.details:
On the grid container div (the one with class grid gap-6 mt-10...), add the HTML attribute role="list". This tells screen readers the grid is a collection of items.

Change from:
<div class="...grid classes...">

To:
<div role="list" class="...grid classes...">

fileChanges.description: Add role='list' to the grid container div in the editor
Add role='listitem' to the card wrapper div
fileChanges.details:
The TextCard wrapper div uses theme_block_props(). Since theme_block_props accepts a second $props parameter for additional HTML attributes, we need to check if it supports arbitrary attributes. If it does, pass ['role' => 'listitem']. Otherwise, add role="listitem" directly to the div element before the theme_block_props output.

Preferred approach: Add role="listitem" as a raw attribute on the <div> element. Since theme_block_props outputs id, class, and aria-labelledby via get_block_wrapper_attributes(), adding role separately is safe:

<div role="listitem" <?php theme_block_props([...]); ?>>

fileChanges.description: Add role='listitem' to the card wrapper div
fileChanges.details:
In useBlockProps call, add role: 'listitem' to the props object.

Change from:
useBlockProps({ className: className({...}) })
To:
useBlockProps({ className: className({...}), role: 'listitem' })

fileChanges.description: Add role='listitem' to the card wrapper div
fileChanges.details:
Same pattern as TextCard -- add role="listitem" to the wrapper div.

<div role="listitem" <?php theme_block_props([...]); ?>>

fileChanges.description: Add role='listitem' to the card wrapper div
fileChanges.details:
In useBlockProps call, add role: 'listitem' to the props object.

Change from:
useBlockProps({ className: className({...}) })
To:
useBlockProps({ className: className({...}), role: 'listitem' })

attributeChanges.description: Custom alt text for the image. When empty, falls back to the media library alt text. Allows authors to provide context-specific alt text or explicitly mark images as decorative.

designDecisions.question: Should the grid use role='list'/role='listitem' or native <ul>/<li> elements?
designDecisions.recommendation: Use role='list' on the grid div and role='listitem' on each card div. This preserves the existing CSS Grid layout without restructuring the HTML. Native <ul>/<li> would require wrapping the grid in a <ul> and each card in an <li>, which adds extra wrapper elements and could interfere with the nth-child CSS selectors that drive the masonry layout. ARIA roles achieve the same semantic result without structural changes.

designDecisions.question: Should the ARIA grid pattern (role='grid' with arrow-key navigation) be used for the card layout?
designDecisions.recommendation: No. The ARIA grid pattern is for interactive grids where users need to navigate between cells using arrow keys (data tables, spreadsheets). MasonryCards is a presentational layout of static content cards. Using role='grid' would require implementing complex keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Home/End, Page Up/Down) that provides no benefit for static cards. The list pattern (role='list') is the correct semantic for a collection of related content items.

designDecisions.question: Should image cards without alt text be hidden from screen readers with aria-hidden?
designDecisions.recommendation: No. Adding aria-hidden='true' would hide the entire card from the accessibility tree, which changes the perceived card count and could be confusing. Instead, provide the alt text mechanism (A11Y-001) and trust content authors to use it appropriately. Images without alt text will render as decorative (alt='') via WordPress default behavior, which is acceptable for background/decorative images in a masonry layout.

designDecisions.question: Should color contrast be enforced at the block level for accent-colored cards?
designDecisions.recommendation: No block-level change. The accent color system is theme-level infrastructure. Each accent color variant should be audited at the theme level to ensure sufficient contrast with body text color. The MasonryCards block correctly uses semantic color tokens (bg-accent) rather than hardcoded colors.

accessibilityPatterns.name: Landmarks
accessibilityPatterns.details: Block uses semantic <section> element. theme_block_props() automatically adds aria-labelledby pointing to the heading ID generated by theme_block_region_id(). This creates a proper labeled region landmark.

accessibilityPatterns.name: Grid (WAI-ARIA)
accessibilityPatterns.details: The ARIA grid pattern is for interactive data/layout grids requiring arrow-key navigation. MasonryCards is a presentational layout of static content cards. The list pattern (role='list'/role='listitem') is more appropriate.

existingAccessibilityFeatures: Automatic aria-labelledby on section referencing the heading (via theme_block_props)
Proper heading hierarchy: h2 for section title, h3 for card titles
ThemeHeading generates unique IDs for heading elements
wp_get_attachment_image() uses media library alt text by default
Keyboard focus is inherently supported (no custom interactive elements that would break tab order)
Content is fully visible without JavaScript (CSS-only masonry layout)
qaFocus: Verify role='list' appears on grid container in rendered HTML
Verify role='listitem' appears on each card wrapper in rendered HTML
Verify image alt text attribute works: custom alt overrides media library alt
Verify empty alt attribute produces alt='' (decorative) on frontend
Screen reader test: verify cards are announced as 'list of N items'
Verify no regressions in masonry layout (nth-child selectors still work with role attributes)
